description: Explains Craft's socket-based multiplayer protocol, how the C client enters online mode, how chunk cache keys and world updates flow through the Python server, and how login, chat, players, time, lights, and signs are synchronized.
document:
Craft's multiplayer mode is intentionally small and direct. The README describes it as plain sockets with an ASCII, line-based protocol, where each newline-terminated line starts with a short command code followed by comma-separated arguments. The C game client owns the player experience and rendering, while the Python server in server.py keeps the shared world state, relays live player updates, and answers chunk requests. The server can be self-hosted with the Python script, but it also depends on the compiled world library used through world.py so it can generate the same default terrain as the client before applying stored edits.

The client enters online mode either from command-line host and port arguments or from the in-game online command handled by parse_command in src/main.c. In both cases the global mode is switched, the destination address is stored, and the local database path becomes a cache file named for that server and port. Returning offline changes the mode back and points the database at either the default save file or a user-provided file. During online initialization the main loop enables the networking layer, connects to the server, starts the receive thread, sends the protocol version, and then runs login.

The networking layer in client.c is a thin wrapper around TCP. Outgoing helpers format protocol lines for authentication, chunk requests, block edits, light changes, signs, chat, position, and version negotiation, but they do nothing unless the client has been enabled. Incoming bytes are collected by a background receive worker into a fixed-size queue protected by a mutex. Each frame, the main loop asks client_recv for complete newline-delimited data and passes any returned buffer to parse_buffer, which may contain more than one protocol line.

Authentication is split between the local client and the server. The client login routine checks the selected identity stored in the local database. If one exists, auth.c contacts the Craft identity endpoint to exchange the identity token for an access token, and the client sends either the authenticated username and access token or an empty guest login. The server's authentication handler verifies non-empty credentials against its access endpoint. A successful response makes the player's nickname the username; otherwise the player remains a guest. The default server configuration requires authentication for building, light edits, and sign edits, although unauthenticated users can still connect as guests.

Chunk synchronization uses cache keys rather than resending an entire chunk delta on every request. request_chunk reads the saved key for a chunk from the local database and asks the server for updates after that key. The server answers by querying block rows newer than the key, sending all lights and signs for the chunk, and, when newer blocks exist, sending a replacement key based on the latest database row. If anything changed, it also asks the client to redraw the chunk. The client applies block, light, and sign messages into the local chunk maps and database, stores replacement keys, and dirties chunks when redraw messages arrive.

The same protocol also keeps the live world coherent. Local block, light, and sign edits update the client-side world immediately, then send compact edit messages to the server. The server validates coordinates, allowed block types, authentication, indestructible blocks, and light or sign constraints before writing to SQLite and broadcasting accepted changes to other clients. Position messages are sent by the client at most about ten times per second and only when movement or rotation changes. The server broadcasts other players' positions, names, disconnects, chat, time, and teleport responses. On the client, parse_buffer interprets these messages by moving the local player when assigned a server identity, adding or updating remote players, deleting disconnected players, updating chat text, and resetting the day timer when the server sends time.
